scenarios.name: 2000s Casual
scenarios.description: Let's put together a casual 2000s look. Let's make it fun, like a girls day at the mall!

scoredItems.commentary: Perfect! Bright colors and flowy fabrics fit in perfectly with boho trends of the 2000s.
That’s right! Miniskirts were a popular casual choice in the 2000s.
Great work! Wedge heels and ankle straps were popular design choices in the 2000s for casual footwear.

incorrectSlotFeedback.top: The 2000s embraced boho styles, consider experimenting with fun colors and flowy shapes!
incorrectSlotFeedback.bottoms: Miniskirts had a resurgence in 2000s, often in fabrics like denim.
incorrectSlotFeedback.shoes: Wedge heels were a popular choice in the 2000s, for both dressy and casual looks.

top.commentary: Bright colors and flowy fabrics fit in perfectly with boho trends of the 2000s.

bottoms.commentary: Miniskirts were a popular casual choice in the 2000s.

shoes.commentary: Wedge heels and ankle straps were popular design choices in the 2000s for casual footwear.

scenarios.name: Hanging Out at a Record Store
scenarios.description: It's 1994 in Seattle. You're meeting friends at a record store to browse for new albums and maybe catch a local band later. Dress the part — grunge is king.

scoredItems.commentary: The open flannel shirt is THE grunge layer — Kurt Cobain's uniform and the defining garment of 90s Seattle culture.
A denim jacket fits the 90s casual vibe well, though flannel is more specifically grunge.
A power blazer? Grunge was a direct rejection of 80s corporate excess. This couldn't be more wrong.
Hoodies existed in the 90s but the zip-up style is more associated with 2000s culture.
An oversized graphic tee was the base layer of every grunge outfit — the more faded and worn, the better.
The babydoll dress was a key piece of 90s grunge fashion for women, especially paired with combat boots.
A turtleneck is very 90s but more minimalist/normcore than grunge. You'd fit in at a gallery, not a mosh pit.
Neon and grunge are mortal enemies. The 90s rejected 80s flash entirely.
A metallic halter top is Y2K, not grunge. Wrong decade, wrong subculture.
Baggy, worn-in jeans were essential to the grunge look — preferably ripped at the knee.
Acid wash is too 80s. Grunge preferred dark or faded natural denim, not processed treatments.
Low-rise jeans are a 2000s trend. In the 90s, waistlines sat higher.
Skinny jeans are the opposite of the 90s baggy silhouette.
Dr. Martens combat boots were the footwear of grunge. Scuffed and worn-in was the ideal.
Platform sneakers are very 90s but more Spice Girls pop than Seattle grunge.
High-tops carry over from the 80s and work casually, but they're not the grunge shoe.
Chunky skate shoes are more early 2000s than mid-90s grunge.

incorrectSlotFeedback.jacket: Grunge layering meant a flannel shirt or denim jacket — nothing structured, nothing shiny, nothing corporate.
incorrectSlotFeedback.top: An oversized, worn-in graphic tee or band shirt was the grunge base layer. Comfort over style, always.
incorrectSlotFeedback.bottoms: Baggy, ripped jeans were non-negotiable. The more worn-in, the more authentic.
incorrectSlotFeedback.shoes: Doc Martens or similar chunky combat boots completed the grunge look.

jacket.commentary: Flannel became a symbol of Pacific Northwest working-class culture adopted by grunge musicians. It represented anti-fashion as fashion.

top.commentary: A thrift-store graphic tee was the foundation. Band tees, vintage finds, and worn-out logos were all authentic choices.

bottoms.commentary: Baggy jeans rejected the fitted styles of the 80s. Grunge embraced shapelessness as a form of comfort and anti-establishment style.

shoes.commentary: Dr. Martens 1460 boots became the unofficial footwear of grunge. Their working-class origins aligned with the subculture's values.

scenarios.name: A Night Out at the Club
scenarios.description: It's 2003 and you're getting ready for a Saturday night at the hottest club in town. Think Paris Hilton, Destiny's Child, and MTV's TRL. Your outfit needs to sparkle.

scoredItems.commentary: A velour track jacket is peak Y2K luxury casual, though you might ditch it once you're inside the club.
A hoodie is too casual for a club night. The 2000s club scene demanded glamour, not comfort.
A leather jacket works for edge, but the 2000s club look was more about flash and bling than punk attitude.
The bomber jacket revival hasn't happened yet — that's a 2010s trend.
A metallic or sequined halter top was THE going-out top of the early 2000s. Maximum skin, maximum sparkle.
A strapless tube dress in a bold print or metallic fabric was standard Y2K club wear. Iconic.
Layered polos with popped collars were a 2000s look, though more frat party than dance floor.
A baggy graphic tee is grunge, not glam. The 2000s club demanded something more eye-catching.
Athleisure hasn't become a thing yet, and workout wear wouldn't fly at a 2003 club.
Ultra low-rise jeans were the defining bottom of the early 2000s, often paired with a visible thong strap (yes, really).
Cargo shorts are daytime casual at best. A nightclub demands something more polished.
Baggy jeans feel too 90s for a 2003 club. The Y2K look was more fitted and flashy.
Skinny jeans won't peak until the 2010s. In 2003, bootcut and flare were king.
UGGs are for the morning after, not the night of. No club would let you in wearing these.
Skate shoes are too casual for a nightclub. The 2000s club scene required heels or pointed-toe shoes.
Platform shoes carry over from the late 90s but proper heels or strappy sandals were the 2003 move.
Stilettos work for a club, but the 80s silhouette is slightly dated. 2000s heels were strappier and more open.

incorrectSlotFeedback.jacket: Y2K club outerwear was about casual luxury — think velour track jackets, cropped jackets, or fur-trimmed boleros.
incorrectSlotFeedback.top: The early 2000s club demanded maximum impact: halter tops, tube tops, sequins, metallics, and lots of exposed skin.
incorrectSlotFeedback.bottoms: Low-rise was non-negotiable in 2003. Bootcut or flared jeans, or a mini skirt, completed the look.
incorrectSlotFeedback.shoes: Strappy heels, pointed-toe boots, or platform sandals were the club footwear of choice in the early 2000s.

jacket.commentary: Juicy Couture velour became a status symbol. The track jacket was worn to the club and taken off at the door — casual luxury personified.

top.commentary: The halter top was the centerpiece of 2000s going-out fashion. Sparkle was mandatory, subtlety was not.

bottoms.commentary: Low-rise jeans defined Y2K fashion. The lower the waistband, the more on-trend you were. Fashion historians still debate how this happened.

shoes.commentary: Platform shoes bridged the late 90s and early 2000s. For a more authentic 2003 look, strappy heeled sandals would be perfect.

scenarios.name: Weekend at a Music Festival
scenarios.description: It's 2015 and you're heading to Coachella. The look is 'effortlessly cool' — streetwear meets festival chic. Comfort matters because you'll be on your feet all day, but style is non-negotiable.

scoredItems.commentary: A lightweight bomber jacket was the go-to festival layer in the mid-2010s — stylish, practical, and Instagram-ready.
An oversized blazer is very 2010s but more suited to a gallery opening than a dusty festival field.
A denim jacket works at any festival and fits the 2010s vintage revival trend, though it's not peak 2015 style.
A leather jacket has festival edge but might be too hot for Coachella's desert setting.
An athleisure tank blends comfort and style perfectly for a festival — very on-trend for 2015.
A longline tee is peak 2015 streetwear — relaxed, stylish, and right at home at Coachella.
A slip dress is a great festival choice for 2015, channeling the 90s revival trend that was huge at the time.
A halter top works for a festival but feels more 2000s than 2010s. The vibe has shifted toward effortless over flashy.
A vintage graphic tee fits the 2010s thrift culture, but the oversized 90s fit isn't quite 2015's silhouette.
Crop tops exist at festivals, but neon spandex reads costume rather than 2015 style.
Skinny jeans were still dominant in 2015, though denim cutoffs might be more practical for a hot festival.
Joggers perfectly capture the 2015 athleisure-meets-streetwear vibe. Comfortable and on-trend.
Low-rise jeans were deeply uncool in 2015. High-waisted everything had taken over.
Baggy jeans hadn't been revived yet in 2015. Slim and tapered silhouettes ruled.
Clean white sneakers were the footwear of the 2010s — simple, versatile, and effortlessly cool at any festival.
Chelsea boots at a festival are a strong 2010s choice — stylish and sturdy enough for the terrain.
Combat boots have festival cred but feel more 90s grunge than 2015 streetwear.
UGGs at Coachella in 2015? Absolutely not. They'd been a fashion punchline for years.
Retro high-tops have some appeal but aren't the 2015 festival shoe of choice.

incorrectSlotFeedback.jacket: 2015 festival outerwear was about lightweight layers — bomber jackets, denim jackets, or a casual kimono.
incorrectSlotFeedback.top: Streetwear-influenced pieces like longline tees, crop tops, or vintage-revival slip dresses defined 2015 festival style.
incorrectSlotFeedback.bottoms: Skinny jeans, joggers, or high-waisted shorts were the go-to. Everything was slim, tapered, or high-waisted.
incorrectSlotFeedback.shoes: Minimalist white sneakers or Chelsea boots — comfortable enough to stand all day, stylish enough for Instagram.

jacket.commentary: The bomber jacket's 2015 revival turned it from military surplus into a streetwear staple. Lightweight versions were perfect for festivals.

top.commentary: The longline tee embodied 2015's streetwear-casual aesthetic. Extended hems and curved cuts were everywhere, from Coachella to city streets.

bottoms.commentary: Joggers represented the athleisure revolution — the idea that comfort and style weren't mutually exclusive.

shoes.commentary: The white sneaker trend peaked in the mid-2010s. Stan Smiths alone became a cultural phenomenon.

scenarios.name: First Day at a New School
scenarios.description: It's 2001 and it's your first day at a new middle school. You want to fit in without trying too hard. The hallways are ruled by TRL, pop-punk, and whatever's at the mall.

scoredItems.commentary: A zip-up hoodie was the universal outerwear of early 2000s teens — worn to school, the mall, and everywhere in between.
A velour track jacket is very 2000s but leans more glamorous than the typical middle school hallway vibe.
Flannel feels leftover from the 90s. By 2001, grunge was over and pop-punk had taken its place.
Bomber jackets won't be trendy again until the 2010s. In 2001, this looks random.
Graphic tees carried over from the 90s and were still everywhere in 2001, though the logos shifted from grunge bands to skate brands.
Layered polos were emerging in the early 2000s, especially in preppy circles. Points for being ahead of the trend.
Band tees are always somewhat in play, but 80s metal bands aren't what middle schoolers were listening to in 2001.
Longline tees are a 2010s streetwear trend. In 2001, this just looks like your shirt is too big.
Cargo shorts with a million pockets were the uniform of 2001 middle school boys. Absolutely essential.
Low-rise jeans were taking over in 2001. For middle school, bootcut low-rise was the default.
Baggy jeans still had a presence in 2001, especially in skate and hip-hop circles, but the silhouette was evolving.
Skinny jeans would get you laughed out of a 2001 middle school. No one was wearing these yet.
Chunky DC or Etnies skate shoes were mandatory footwear in 2001, whether or not you actually owned a skateboard.
UGGs were just starting to appear in 2001, mainly among girls. They hadn't fully exploded yet.
Minimalist white sneakers are a 2010s aesthetic. In 2001, sneakers needed to be chunky and colorful.
Platform sneakers are late 90s. By 2001 they were falling out of favor.

incorrectSlotFeedback.jacket: A zip-up hoodie was the default outerwear for early 2000s teens. Simple, comfortable, and universally accepted.
incorrectSlotFeedback.top: Graphic tees with skate brand logos, band names, or funny slogans were standard. Nothing too dressy, nothing too plain.
incorrectSlotFeedback.bottoms: For boys: cargo shorts or baggy jeans. For girls: low-rise bootcut jeans. Comfort and brand logos mattered most.
incorrectSlotFeedback.shoes: Chunky skate shoes from DC, Etnies, or Vans were the footwear that every kid wanted in 2001.

jacket.commentary: The zip-up hoodie was democratically cool — skaters, preps, and everyone in between wore one. Brand mattered (Billabong, Hurley, Element) but the basic form was universal.

top.commentary: A graphic tee under the hoodie was standard layering. In 2001, the tees featured skate logos, video game references, or pop-punk bands.

bottoms.commentary: Cargo shorts represented early 2000s practicality-as-fashion. The more pockets, the better — even if you never used them.

shoes.commentary: Skate shoes with fat tongues and padded collars were non-negotiable. The brand on your feet was a social signal in middle school.
